summary: Jeff Atwood explores replacing a dedicated router with a cheap, powerful mini-PC running standard Linux. After finding his aging Asus router's firmware increasingly outdated, he discovered that ~$350 Broadwell mini-PCs from AliExpress offer remarkable specs: i5 CPUs, 16GB RAM, SSDs, and dual gigabit ethernet. Benchmarks show these 'scooter computers' match or exceed typical cloud VMs and dramatically outperform the weak Atom CPUs found in expensive dedicated networking hardware. The passively-cooled, fanless design proved stable even under extreme CPU torture tests. Atwood concludes that commodity x86 hardware running standard Linux distributions is now a better choice than purpose-built networking appliances with custom firmware.
key_points: Cheap AliExpress mini-PCs (~$350) with real i5 CPUs outperform both cloud VMs and expensive dedicated networking hardware using weak Atom processors
Passive cooling of a 15W TDP x86 CPU is achievable but requires careful thermal management - the entire aluminum case acts as a heatsink
The 'scooter' metaphor captures the appeal: simple, minimal maintenance, just works - primitive machines whose simplicity is both strength and weakness
Purpose-built networking hardware like pfSense appliances ($1,400) use underpowered old Atom cores that can't compete with modern consumer mini-PCs
Standard Linux distributions on commodity x86 are easier to maintain and update than specialized router firmware like TomatoUSB
money_quotes: This is just a little box that runs a chopped up version of Linux, with a bit of specialized wireless hardware and multiple antennas tacked on… that we're not even using.
Why not just go with a small box that can run a real, full Linux distro? Wouldn't that be simpler and easier to keep up to date?
This isn't a Steve Jobs truck, a Mac Mini car, or even a motorcycle. This is a scooter.
Passively cooled computers are paragons of simplicity and reliable consumer electronics, but passively cooling a 'real' x86 PC is the holy grail.
If your CPU can survive an overnight run of mprime, I can assure you it's ready for just about anything the real world can throw at it, ever.
You're often better off building up a modern high power, low TDP x86 box and slapping a regular Linux distro on there.
Sure, 2.4 Ghz and 8 cores on that C2758 sounds reasonable – until you realize those are old Intel Bay Trail Atom cores.
key_insight: Commodity x86 mini-PCs running standard Linux have become so cheap and powerful that they obsolete expensive purpose-built networking hardware with weak CPUs and hard-to-maintain custom firmware.
